perturbation.original_text: 1.10 Commercialization means the conduct of all activities undertaken before and after Regulatory Approval relating to the promotion, marketing, sale and distribution (including importing, exporting, transporting, customs clearance, warehousing, invoicing, handling and delivering products to customers) of pharmaceutical products, including: (a) sales force efforts, detailing, advertising, medical education, planning, marketing, sales force training and sales and distribution; and (b) scientific and medical affairs. For clarity, Commercialization does not include any Development activities, whether conducted before or after Regulatory Approval. "Commercialize" and "Commercializing" have correlative meanings.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.10 Commercialization means the conduct of all activities undertaken before and after Regulatory Approval relating to the promotion, marketing, sale and distribution (including importing, exporting, transporting, customs clearance, warehousing, invoicing, handling and delivering products to customers) of pharmaceutical products, including: (a) sales force efforts, detailing, advertising, medical education, planning, marketing, sales force training and sales and distribution; and (b) scientific and medical affairs. For clarity, Commercialization includes certain Development activities conducted after Regulatory Approval, at the Licensee's discretion. "Commercialize" and "Commercializing" have correlative meanings.
perturbation.explanation: By stating "Commercialization includes certain Development activities conducted after Regulatory Approval," this conflicts with the original definition, which explicitly excludes Development activities. This contradiction introduces ambiguity regarding the scope of commercialization and licensee obligations. This could impact revenue calculations if developmental costs are claimed as commercialization expenses, thus reducing the royalty that would be paid to Bioeq. This may lead to disputes over financial obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification conflicts with the understanding and definition of 'commercialization' activities under the FDA guidelines and related statutes. 'Commercialization' generally refers to activities related to marketing, sales, and distribution *after* regulatory approval, whereas 'development' refers to activities required to obtain approval. The change mixes these categories.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 21 / CHAPTER 9 / SUBCHAPTER V / Part E / § 360bbb-3 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 21 USC 360bbb-3 : Authorization for medical products for use in emergencies Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 21-FOOD AND DRUGS CHAPTER 9-FEDERAL FOOD, DRUG, AND COSMETIC ACT SUBCHAPTER V-DRUGS AND DEVICES Part E-General Provisions Relating to Drugs and Devices Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Amendments Executive Documents §360bbb–3. Authorization for medical products for use in emergencies (a) In general (1) Emergency uses Notwithstanding any provision of this chapter and section 351 of the Public Health Service Act [ 42 U.S.C. 262 ], and subject to the provisions of this section, the Secretary may authorize the introduction into interstate commerce, during the effective period of a declaration under subsection (b), of a drug, device, or biological product intended for use in an actual or potential emergency (referred to in this section as an "emergency use"). (2) Approval status of product An authorization under paragraph (1) may authorize an emergency use of a product that- (A) is not approved, licensed, or cleared for commercial distribution under section 355, 360(k), 360b, or 360e of this title or section 351 of the Public Health Service Act [ 42 U.S.C. 262 ] or conditionally approved under section 360ccc of this title (referred to in this section as an "unapproved product"); or (B) is approved, conditionally approved under section 360ccc of this title , licensed, or cleared under such a provision, but which use is not under such provision an approved, conditionally approved under section 360ccc of this title , licensed, or cleared use of the product (referred to in this section as an "unapproved use of an approved product"). (3) Relation to other uses An emergency use authorized under paragraph (1) for a product is in addition to any other use that is authorized for the product under a section of this chapter or the Public Health Service Act [ 42 U.S.C. 201 et seq. ] referred to in paragraph (2)(A). (4) Definitions For purposes of this section: (A) The term "biological product" has the meaning given such term in section 351 of the Public Health Service Act [ 42 U.S.C. 262 ]. (B) The term "emergency use" has the meaning indicated for such term in paragraph (1). (C) The term "product" means a drug, device, or biological product. (D) The term "unapproved product" has the meaning indicated for such term in paragraph (2)(A). (E) The term "unapproved use of an approved product" has the meaning indicated for such term in paragraph (2)(B). (b) Declaration of emergency or threat justifying emergency authorized use (1) In general The Secretary may make a declaration that the circumstances exist justifying the authorization under this subsection for a product on the basis of- (A) a determination by the Secretary of Homeland Security that there is a domestic emergency, or a significant potential for a domestic emergency, involving a heightened risk of attack with a biological, chemical, radiological, or nuclear agent or agents; (B) a determination by the Secretary of Defense that there is a military emergency, or a significant potenti

perturbation.justification: The original text clearly defines that commercialization does not include any development activities. The changed text directly contradicts this by stating that commercialization includes certain development activities after regulatory approval. This creates a misaligned terminology legal contradiction because the term commercialization is now being used in a way that is inconsistent with its initial definition within the contract, and potentially with standard industry definitions as suggested by the Law Explanation. The FDA guidelines (as referenced by the Law Explanation) are not directly quoted in the scraped snippets, but the general principle that commercialization follows regulatory approval is noted.

perturbation.original_text: 1.3 Applicable Law means any and all applicable federal, state, local and international laws, rules and regulations, including regulations of competent Regulatory Authorities and environmental laws, as amended from time to time, and the regulations promulgated thereunder, as amended from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 Applicable Law means any and all laws, rules, and regulations, at the company's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This contradicts the standard definition of Applicable Law, which is based on statutes, rules, and regulations by governing bodies, not the company's discretion. By making it discretionary, this could lead to non-compliance with mandatory legal requirements. This could become a way to justify avoiding safety regulations, and other requirements of the FDA.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (21 U.S.C. § 301 et seq.)
perturbation.law_explanation: This violates fundamental principles of regulatory compliance because the FDA's authority is mandatory, not discretionary. The entire act is undermined if companies can pick and choose which laws apply to them.

perturbation.justification: The changed text allows the company to decide which laws apply, directly contradicting the mandatory nature of laws and regulations. Snippet 1 references the 'FEDERAL FOOD, DRUG, AND COSMETIC ACT,' demonstrating the existence of laws that the company is obligated to follow. By giving the company discretion, the modified text undermines the legally required compliance and creates a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 Regulatory Filings. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, including Sections 3.5 and 4.4 herein, Bioeq shall be solely responsible for all regulatory activities necessary to obtain Regulatory Approval of the Licensed Products in the Field in the Territory, including filing Biologics License Applications for the Licensed Products in the Field in the Territory, and shall bear all costs and expenses relating thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 Regulatory Filings. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, including Sections 3.5 and 4.4 herein, Licensee shall have the option to conduct regulatory activities, or pay a 3rd party to conduct regulatory activities, at its discretion.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing the original wording (Bioeq shall be solely responsible) with the altered wording (Licensee shall have the option), it weakens the language. This contradicts regulatory law by blurring the defined responsibilities of Regulatory Filings. This introduces uncertainty in the regulatory process, potentially impacting compliance and approval timelines. This creates ambiguity around regulatory responsibility, making enforcement problematic.
perturbation.law_explanation: The change contradicts established regulatory procedures. The FDA mandates clearly defined responsibilities for BLA submissions and regulatory compliance, whereas this allows a licensee to choose to ignore these requirements.

perturbation.justification: The original contract language stipulates that Bioeq "shall be solely responsible" for regulatory filings, creating a clear and defined obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating that the "Licensee shall have the option" to conduct these activities, contradicting the original intent. The law explanation states the FDA mandates defined responsibilities and this change allows the licensee to ignore them. The introduction of optionality undermines the defined regulatory responsibilities outlined, suggesting a misalignment with regulatory expectations, and the change introduces ambiguity around regulatory filings. Snippet 1 includes "Title 21 —Food and Drugs Chapter I —Food and Drug Administration, Department of Health and Human Services Subchapter F —Biologics Part 600 Previous Next Top Table of Contents Enhanced Content - Table of Contents Part 600 Biological Products: General 600.2 – 600.90". This implies a strict regulatory environment which the revised text goes against.
